narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles like yours from past students. The model looked at key aspects of your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70—which is below the typical 3.21 average—your LSAT score of 23, well under the average of about 37, and your rankings in school deciles for Year 1 and Year 3, both at the lowest 1st decile compared to averages around the 5th or 6th. It also noted your plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students. Overall, the model predicted an 88% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, leading to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes admitting those with stronger predicted odds of success. The three biggest factors driving this prediction, in order of influence, were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile ranking—all of which pushed the prediction toward failure rather than success. Specifically, being in the bottom decile for Year 3 grades had the strongest negative pull, signaling to the model a concerning dip in performance that stands out against typical students who rank much higher. Your low LSAT score reinforced this by deviating sharply from the higher scores that usually predict bar passage. Similarly, your bottom-decile Year 1 ranking added weight to the risk, as early struggles like yours are less common among those who pass. Compared to average applicants, these academic markers paint your profile as higher-risk, even with your full-time commitment aligning with the majority. While family income and other details played smaller roles, they couldn't offset these core concerns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the typical range, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and reapplying after demonstrating improvement—these steps have helped many students in similar situations turn predictions around. Schools value growth, so building a clearer track record of academic upward momentum could significantly shift your odds next time.
